macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish on Missing Shirayim Between Kemitza and Haktara

micro_segments.title: Setting up the debate: Reish Lakish invalidates, Rabbi Yochanan permits offering the kometz despite missing shirayim
Rav Pappa's report of the debate and the question about whether it concerns minchat ha'omer of the tzibur
The proof from Lechem HaPanim: offering the bazichin and levona even with missing parts supports Rabbi Yochanan
Two braitot from separate pesukim teaching the problem of chaser in different scenarios of mincha
Resolving: first pesuk applies to lacking before kemitza; second pesuk concerns shirayim lacking between kemitza and haktara
The dispute whether shirayim can be eaten when some went missing between kemitza and haktara

macro_segments.title: The Requirement of Right Hand for Kemitza: Derivation from the Word Kaf and the Metzora Case

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: kemitza done with left hand is invalid
Rabbi Zeira's derivation: kaf without modifier means right hand, learned from the metzora case requiring explicitly 'left palm'
Gemara resolves ambiguity about the redundancy of 'left' appearing multiple times in the metzora passages
Rabbi Yirmiya's challenge: if left teaches right by default, what about all the explicit mentions of right in the metzora case?
Rava's elegant solution: the repetitions of right, right, right in the metzora case teach that right is required for kemitza, chalitza, and ear piercing

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Shimon and the Chachamim on Which Actions Require the Right Hand

micro_segments.title: Rabbah bar bar Chana's principle: whenever 'finger' and 'kehuna' are both mentioned, the right hand is required
Challenge: bringing limbs to the ramp mentions kehuna but is done with both hands, not only right
Gemara clarifies: the principle applies only to essential avoda (services that invalidate the offering if done with left hand)
The case of kabbalat hadam (catching blood): essential avoda, mentions kehuna; Chachamim require right, Rabbi Shimon permits left
Rabbi Shimon's principle: only when 'finger' (etzba) or 'hand' (yad) is explicitly mentioned does right become required; kehuna alone does not demand it
Dispute over zrika (throwing blood): Chachamim invalidate if done with left; Rabbi Shimon permits left hand
Resolving multiple derivations: Rava's yad yad (from metzora) and the Chachamim's kehuna principle can both apply to different aspects of kemitza
Rabbi Shimon's teaching on minchat choté: if using the hand directly (not a vessel), right hand is required like chattat; if using a vessel, left hand is permitted like asham
